rationale: Navarro-Martinez et al. is cited in multiple functional locations: in the introduction to position the limited prior debt-behavior literature, in theory development to support the claim that payment requirements alter psychological representation, and in the discussion of policy implications. These are not just list citations; the paper uses the cited study to frame why representation of debt matters behaviorally. However, the citing paper does not directly reuse the cited paper's method or test its specific disclosure interventions, so the link is substantive but best categorized as a non-standard relation.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Navarro-Martinez et al. (2011) as an important adjacent precedent showing that credit-card statement/payment framing affects repayment behavior. It leverages that precedent to motivate its own argument that cognitive representation of debt influences behavior, while shifting the focal outcome from repayment to spending.
